item.title: Late to the party, but...
item.content:encoded: Honestly, I really like where this one ended up. I didn't read the original from a few years ago, but the current iteration is interesting, leaves just enough in the dark, and doesn't overstay its welcome. +1, nice revisory work and nice original concept as well :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Do you mean to say that none of the remembered rituals are re-creatable?
Because the write-up doesn't actually say that.

The two examples we get happen to not be re-doable; the first because the specific date has passed and the second because the date is nonsense. But it doesn't necessarily follow that all the memories are non-actionable. In fact we are told that some of the scenarios are downright "plausible".

We slaughtered the fatted calf and anointed the twice-forgiven murderer with blood and bile. Then under the first full moon of spring, the living spirit of the anointed was sent skyward on a pyre of ash, yew, and elm. His physical body was consigned to the mother waters. But to our shame and horror, we made a mistake and our doom yet approaches.
We have 11 months to prepare to do better.

Edit: DERP, intended as a reply to Voct.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
They'll try strangling three blind defrocked priests with cords made of their own tendons while chanting 'Happy Birthday' again when it's the right time. If you do it at the wrong time, it won't work.

When is the right time?

… not yet.

When will it be the right time?

They're not sure. They'll know.

Except a bunch of ducks landed in the pond this morning, and two of them got in a fight and there's feathers all over the surface of the water, and now the information about that ritual is gone forever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The affected all believe that A) the ritual is/was necessary to save the world, and B) that the ritual was botched the first time, and C) the sacrifice goes into the lake as the last step.

So it makes sense that most people seeing the lake for the first time would immediately scramble to try the ritual "again". After all, the world is still in danger! Some won't be able to get anywhere with their ritual1. But some folks would be making a beeline for the nearest poorly supervised kindergarten.
The remembered first rounds are all false, but all those second attempts would be very real and have left real victims in the lake.

+ show block
– hide block

So why didn't The Foundation find any remains?
Something else must be happening that they overlooked.

Then consider that 1/3 of the "false" doomsdays are plausible scenarios that The Foundation knows could actually happen. Maybe the other 70% are equally possible, but involve anomalies that haven't been found yet.

My headcanon is that the "false" memories are all real, just not from the current reality.
When the cultists properly buggered and drowned that VW, they didn't actually prevent SCP-XXXX from releasing swarms of needle bats to blot out the sun. But they did kick reality over to a parallel track, a timeline where that apocalypse isn't looming yet.
There isn't a car in the lake because the ritual didn't happen in this world. The green beetle is at the bottom of the lake in the reality that got left behind.

That also might explain why some of the rituals are nonsense. The rules were slightly different in the reality that used to be. Certain doomsdays are logically inescapable, so the only way out is to go where logic is different.

Of course, that would mean that by preventing the rituals from being done "again", and properly this time, The Foundation is actively interfering with a process that has spared the world from countless Armageddon's.


As a courtesy to our readers on mobile devices, please collapse long posts. ~Zyn
Footnotes
1. Although, with that strong a motivation, we can expect some pretty imaginative workarounds. Fleshlights bolted into exhaust pipes, that sort of thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted for "reports of livestock theft…" and its implications. In general this is compelling but there's just some level of menace that it doesn't have. I wish I had something more constructive than that but I don't.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seconding this. I enjoyed reading this one a lot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The first half of this is pretty rote, but:

strangulation of a green 1978 Volkswagen Beetle, on the fifty-sixth day of June, 1974

Upvoted here, like a lot of people. (The rape part seemed shoehorned in, and boring, but the rest was still great.)

Also the possibility of the end of the world scenarios being real things confirmed my upvote, making it more than just a plain delusion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: First voct rewrite i dont love, but its decent so novote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh wow, Voct really saved this one long, long ago. I loved "the rape and strangulation of a green 1978 Volkswagen Beetle" though I'm slightly miffed that that was just created wholesale in the midst of musing about how to rewrite. Still a great idea, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Awoke my interest, but needs more meat. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes. And if we show you a video of you describing how it was "just me and Alice and five hundred newborn puppies in a pentagram made of their umbilical cords", you'll say "yeah, that's exactly it - oh, except for Bob, he was there too, I forgot to mention him".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: With the "participants" being aware of other "ritual participants" thing: Supposing I see the lake and think I drowned all puppies in the world in it, and prior to me looking into the lake, only Alice has looked into it. So, I'll think I drowned the puppies with Alice's help. But then Bob comes along and looks into the lake and thinks he's drowned all the puppies with my and Alice's help. Do my memories of doing the ritual now incorporate Bob in there?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted for Volkswagen beetle rape. That victims hallucinate a seemingly ad-libbed "atrocity" makes this for me and sends the possibilities skyrocketing…not sure if I'd prefer it left simple as it is or see a log of many different perceived rituals.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Tell you what. Write up an interview, post it on the talk page; if I like it, I'll incorporate it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I dunno. I like how it is now. An interview log might take away from the "Holy crap, this could happen to me" feel, imho.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't know…. I feel like it kinda needs more of a kick, it's kinda just….. too passive. I was kinda hoping for an interview.

Or can I write one?

item.title: Re: Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Deletion vote cancelled.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm digging the concept, but I think it still needs some tightening up… phrases like the following strike me as off:

all affected subjects express intense remorse and guilt at having participated (regardless of whether this is compatible with their psychological profiles), describe the ritual as having concluded with a victim or victims being submerged within SCP-1464, claim that the ritual was necessary in order to prevent what is effectively an XK-class end-of-the-world scenario, and state that the worst part is that the ritual was incorrectly performed and must be repeated.

It gets the point across to me, but I feel like it can be conveyed in a neater way than that. Anyway I like that the idea is that the pond is giving people false memories, especially nonsensical ones, and that it can be altered by subtle changes in context. I'd even be happy if it was wrapped up as a neutralized thing where the Foundation decided they had milked as much experimentation as they could out of it and turned it into a non-anomalous parking lot or ditch.

item.title: Re: Prelude to a rewrite
item.content:encoded: Any word on that, Voct?

item.title: Re: Prelude to a rewrite
item.content:encoded: Yeah, sorry. I'll do that today.

item.title: Re: Prelude to a rewrite
item.content:encoded: Could we get an update on this?

item.title: Re: Prelude to a rewrite
item.content:encoded: Hmm. Not exactly what I had in mind, but I like it! Ha, it'll probably go over way better than my original idea :p have at, I can't wait to see the finished version!

item.title: Re: Prelude to a rewrite
item.content:encoded:
See what you can do, Voct.

I think someone of your skills can pull it off.

item.title: Prelude to a rewrite
item.content:encoded:
Okay, let's start by outlining the basic idea.

It's a body of water, with an anomalous memetic thingy: specifically, viewing the exact shape of the coastline, etc, induces false memories in you that you once participated in a huge ritual atrocity at that lake. And all subjects uniformly state that the ritual was done wrong and will have to be repeated to prevent (something).

The idea that the lake actually is full of evidence-of-atrocities kind of breaks this.

What I propose is that this memetic effect is very delicate. If the coastline of the lake (no, it's a pond instead?) changes even a tiny bit, then the nature of the memetic induction changes.

The first batch of subjects believed that they participated in an atrocity involving the ritual slaughter of nine nine-year-old girls, at nine minutes past nine AM, on the ninth day of the ninth month, nineteen ninety-nine.

Then one of the remote sampler bots (because researchers can't take samples themselves without being affected) accidentally snapped a small tree in half, and it fell in the pond and uprooted a bunch of cattails.

Suddenly all the subjects who see the lake are convinced that they participated in an ritual atrocity involving the group sodomy of a bright green Volkswagen, on the fifty-eighth day of July, 1979. And they're convinced of this even when you point out to them that July doesn't have fifty-eight days, and that anyway they weren't even born in 1979.

In the time that the Foundation has been controlling the area, we've managed to induce seventeen separate changes in the nature of the memetic effect, and another twelve have occurred naturally. They all involve induction of false memories of participation in a ritual "atrocity" at the pond. The goal of the ritual is always described as the prevention of what is effectively an XK-class end-of-the-world scenario; also, the "participants" always feel enormous guilt about their "actions" even when this is grossly counter to their psychological profiles, and they always say that the worst part is that they did it wrong and they'll have to do it again.

Some of the alleged rituals are utterly ridiculous and totally impossible. Some are quite simple and feasible (if grossly immoral and unethical). None of the affected subjects ever actually try to enact their ritual, though, because it's never the right time to do the ritual.

What do you think?

item.title: Re: Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
I give my permission for the rewrite, as long as it doesn't completely gut the concept and stuff I've already got. Go for it. (Please don't entirely omit my character, Dr. Wing, if it's possible. w/e though if it flows better without her.)

Ugh /me trying not to be overbearing JUST DO WHAT YOU WANT, lol.

item.title: Re: Staff Post
item.content:encoded: If the author(s) give permission, I'll grant a stay of deletion on this.

item.title: Re: Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
I think I see how to fix this, but I'm going to be spending most of tomorrow getting ready to travel, and then traveling.

Can I have a few days in which to prepare a rewrite?

item.title: Re: Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Thirding.

item.title: Re: Staff Post
item.content:encoded: seconding.

item.title: Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Beginning deletion vote at -13.

item.title: Going for a different angle
item.content:encoded:
Roserising and I consulted on an idea that we both kinda liked, and it's now hinted at the article. It's a very risky idea, admittedly, and it'll most likely be a make-or-break deal for some, and if you already don't like this it might not change your opinion either way, but we thought we'd at least give it a try.

edit: sorry rose

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think you have the bare basics of a great article. An infectious ritual is an interesting idea, but I feel the execution is just kinda bland.

item.title: Rewrite complete!
item.content:encoded: I've completely rewritten much of the page (only the interview was left unscathed), so I hope people will take a look at it (and hopefully like what they see). I took all of the criticism I've gotten into account. Thank you all for giving me the opportunity. I hope that it's much better now. Please, please let me know if there's anything I can or should do to improve it further.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You have a really good point. I'm going to change that.

item.title: Re: Mod Post
item.content:encoded: I've rewritten most of the description, and added quite a lot to it. Please take another look if you'd be so inclined. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Done! Thank you. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why not just give a Class A to anyone who encounters it, instead of terminating them? Seems a little drastic when according to your containment procedures and interview's closing statement a Class A seems to be completely effective. Easier to give someone amnesiacs, then release them unharmed, rather than terminate them, especially when they're civilians; the Foundation would probably have to work harder to cover up mysterious disappearances of civilians than confused, "lost" civilians.

item.title: Mod Post
item.content:encoded:
I am officially calling for a review of this article. Please read this and, if you are so inclined, vote on it and post your thoughts here.

It hasn't been here for long, but it hasn't got discussion for the last three days, and it's not low enough to get deleted except to wait until it sinks down further. So I think getting this reviewed would be a good use of our time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you! :) I really appreciate it. I'm going to try and take the time to rewrite it tomorrow — I was going to do it yesterday but stuff came up. I hope I can make this work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sounds great! I'll wait to vote till then, in that case.

If it helps, I really like your log. The rest of it just needs cleanup as others have already described, and this could work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Your first priority should be to reword this:
"SCP-1464-2 is to be terminated as soon as it becomes possible to identify and neutralize them".
Otherwise the containment part is good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just in case anyone is wondering, I'm planning on doing a rewrite of this in a few days. Please be patient with me until then. If anyone has any ideas on how to improve the concept or writing, please please let me know! Thanks. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Better, but still doesn't work for me.

There are a few places that you could tighten up the prose. For example, in the first line you could cut the bit before "SCP-1464". Shorter is always better around here, but I don't think that's what's keeping me from digging this. It might be that I'm not successfully separating my first impression from the current version. Sorry I can't be more helpful at this point.

I disagree about the black-boxing. In my opinion, censoring should be kept to an absolute minimum. Every redaction and edit should be considered carefully, even to a letter by letter basis, and only used if there is a strong story-based (prefereably in-universe) reason for it to be unseen. By that standard, redacting names works, but victim count does not. This is just my take, other opinions exist, YMMV.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Kind of an interesting concept, but I'm in the "execution isn't quite there" boat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you for so much feedback. I have edited accordingly. I didn't get rid of all the black-boxing as I felt some of it made sense (and some of it I think helps with the tone), but I tried to tone it down a lot. Please let me know if I was successful. :)

And you're definitely right about the higher-ups thing. I apologize. In the original draft of this, all components of the lake retained their memetic effect once removed from it.. I will edit to reflect that.

ETA: And finished the edit. Please let me know if it looks better now. :) Thank you!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Still too much black-boxing. All the numbers (victim count, percent who spread info, etc.) should be visible because there is no in-universe reason to censor them and out-of-universe it makes for less pleasant reading.

I'm not sure if this is proper use of "memetic", but that is always a matter of debate, so it is whatever it is.

"Northern ██████" shouldn't be capitalized if it is a region. It would only be capitalized if it was part of the full, proper name; ie: North Korea, South Dakota.

Semi-colon before "Frequently" should be a period.

The effect attaches to images of the lake, so why can't higher-ups know about this? As long as they don't have picutes of the site, there is no risk. Same for forensic analysis of the remains. The divers who go and get them would be infected, but once the boens are out of the area they wouldn't have any anomaly. Either tweak the description to be more inclusive of things other than the location or cut those sections.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Misuse of "effect". Thirty-yard penalty.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, sorry :( I did read it, but I did the tags at like 3:30am and slipped up. Sorry!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I fixed your tags for you. Next time, please read the Tag Guide, as you misused several.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Kinda similar to a few other SCPs, like Fluid Canon or the one where people take a picture of the skyline in a city (I think Boston? Or Chicago? Maybe Seattle) and then feel compelled to research a mysterious tower and congregate to other affected individuals. Wish I could remember the name or number of that one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You're definitely right about the designations. I trimmed a bunch and hopefully it's better-looking now. I also changed the "formerly known as" to black-boxing.

The [DATA EXPUNGED] there was something I missed in editing - it was meant to come out already. I fixed it now.

Thank you for your critique :) I appreciate it. Please let me know if I've missed anything in my edits that would make this better!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you for your critique. I've edited for tone and removed the picture, as well as taking out the unnecessary [DATA REDACTED] and so forth. I hope it reads better to you now - please let me know if there's anything I can do to improve it further.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you! :) I've edited for tone and streamlined a bit, hopefully it's even better now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the critique! I changed the wording around and eliminated some of the unnecessary [DATA EXPUNGED] and [REDACTED] stuff. I hope the flow is much better now. :)

I removed the picture as well… It had been the suggestion of someone on the chat and you're right, it doesn't fit that well.

Amnesiacs only "cure" infection by making the subject forget the memetic contagion, so they won't actively seek to re-create the massacre. However, they're still identified by other exposed subjects as having participated and are especially vulnerable to re-exposure. I hope that helps.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Definitely some interesting ideas - the designation of the various elements is slightly confusing though; not sure why you wouldn't refer to the area as SCP-1464, people or groups of people affected as -1, and note that viewing photographs or video footage has the same effect. I will also second soullesshuman's thoughts about expungement, especially as in the Description it isn't warranted at all:

"Formerly known as [DATA REDACTED]" - you've black-boxed the country, so perhaps this should also be similarly shown as redacted rather than stated redacted?
"SCP-1464-3 will attempt to re-create [DATA EXPUNGED]" - unless I'm missing something what's been expunged is simply the words 'the massacre'.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting premise, something that compels people to commit a kind of organized mass murder. The execution makes this all very dull, with all the 'exciting' bits either missing or censored. Also [DATA REDACTED] pisses me off. I also don't know why a picture is there, I think an article like this would call for no picture, but that may just be my personal opinion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It has potential, I kind of liked it. I haven't seen an SCP like this before, so cautiously upvoting to give the author some time to rewrite, maybe cut it down and streamline it a bit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "SCP-1464-3 is to be terminated as soon as it is possible to do so, as a high priority." Just awkwardly phrased, either leave out "as a high priority" or "as soon as it is possible to do so" it would flow better. Also, your used of black out and [ REDACTED ] & [ DATA EXPUNGED ] really break up the flow in a way I found distracting. I also think the picture is unnessecary, since it is explained in the text why one is unavailable. Also do amnesiacs cure infection or not, I can't tell from the piece.
